([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Jeux olympiques d'été de 1920***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): ***Olympische Zomerspelen van 1920***; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Olympische Sommerspiele 1920***), officially known as the **Games of the VII Olympiad** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Jeux de la VIIe olympiade***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): ***Spelen van de VIIe Olympiade***; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Spiele der VII. Olympiade***) and commonly known as **Antwerp 1920** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Anvers 1920***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Antwerpen 1920***), were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in 1920 in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), Belgium.

The **1920 Summer Olympics** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Jeux olympiques d'été de 1920***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): ***Olympische Zomerspelen van 1920***; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Olympische Sommerspiele 1920***), officially known as the **Games of the VII Olympiad** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Jeux de la VIIe olympiade***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): ***Spelen van de VIIe Olympiade***; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Spiele der VII. Olympiade***) and commonly known as **Antwerp 1920** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): ***Anvers 1920***; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): ***Antwerpen 1920***), were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in 1920 in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), Belgium.

In March 1912, during the 13th session of the IOC, Belgium's bid to host the 1920 Summer Olympics was made by Baron [Édouard de Laveleye](/wiki/%C3%89douard_de_Laveleye "Édouard de Laveleye"), president of the Belgian Olympic Committee and of the [Royal Belgian Football Association](/wiki/Royal_Belgian_Football_Association "Royal Belgian Football Association"). No fixed host city was proposed at the time.

The [1916 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1916_Summer_Olympics "1916 Summer Olympics"), to have been held in Berlin, capital of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), were cancelled due to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). When the Olympic Games resumed after the war, Antwerp was awarded the hosting rights to the 1920 Summer Games as a tribute to the Belgian people. The [aftermath of the war](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I") and the [Paris Peace Conference, 1919](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919") affected the Olympic Games not only due to new states being created, but also by sanctions against the nations that lost the war and were blamed for starting it. [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%9346) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–46)"), [Germany](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), [Austria](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria"), and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") were banned from competing in the Games. [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") had just emerged from the [Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War") and chose not to attend the Games. Germany did not return to Olympic competition until [1928](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics") and instead hosted a series of games called [Deutsche Kampfspiele](/wiki/Deutsche_Kampfspiele "Deutsche Kampfspiele"), starting with the Winter edition of 1922 (which predated the first Winter Olympics).

The United States won the most gold and overall medals at the 1920 Summer Games.

## Host city selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1920_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host city selection")]

In March 1912, during the 13th [session of the IOC](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings"), the bid on behalf of Belgium to host the 1920 Summer Olympics was made by Baron [Édouard de Laveleye](/wiki/%C3%89douard_de_Laveleye "Édouard de Laveleye"), president of the [Belgian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Belgian_Olympic_Committee "Belgian Olympic Committee") and of the [Royal Belgian Football Association](/wiki/Royal_Belgian_Football_Association "Royal Belgian Football Association"). No fixed host city was proposed at the time.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The organizing committee was created on 9 August 1913. It had four presidents:

* Édouard de Laveleye, president of the Belgian Olympic Committee
* [Henri de Baillet-Latour](/wiki/Henri_de_Baillet-Latour "Henri de Baillet-Latour"), member of the [IOC](/wiki/IOC "IOC")
* [Robert Osterrieth](/w/index.php?title=Robert_Osterrieth&action=edit&redlink=1 "Robert Osterrieth (page does not exist)"), president of the [Royal Yacht Club of Belgium](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Yacht_Club_of_Belgium&action=edit&redlink=1 "Royal Yacht Club of Belgium (page does not exist)")
* [Charles Cnoops](/w/index.php?title=Charles_Cnoops&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles Cnoops (page does not exist)"), vice-president of the [Belgian Fencing Association](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Fencing_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belgian Fencing Association (page does not exist)")

Among the 22 vice-presidents of the committee were people with a military or industrial background, and further people from sports organizations like [Paul Havenith](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Havenith&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paul Havenith (page does not exist)"), president of the football and athletics club [K. Beerschot V.A.C.](/wiki/K._Beerschot_V.A.C. "K. Beerschot V.A.C.") and [Nicolaas Jan Cupérus](/w/index.php?title=Nicolaas_Jan_Cup%C3%A9rus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicolaas Jan Cupérus (page does not exist)"), president of the [Belgian Gymnastics Federation](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Gymnastics_Federation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belgian Gymnastics Federation (page does not exist)").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The first action of the committee was to send an official letter to the IOC in Paris, confirming Antwerp as the city for the Belgian Olympic bid. With Antwerp confirmed as the Olympic Games host, Belgium began reconstructing the Beerschot Stadium into the [Olympisch Stadion](/wiki/Olympisch_Stadion_(Antwerp) "Olympisch Stadion (Antwerp)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Construction on the new Olympic stadium began in July 1919 and finished in May 1920.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1914, a 109-page brochure was created to promote the idea of Antwerp as a host city for the Olympics: *Aurons-nous la VIIème Olympiade à Anvers?* (*Will we have the 7th Olympiad at Antwerp?*). It was sent to all IOC members and was used during the [6th Olympic Congress](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings") in Paris in 1914, where the candidacies of Amsterdam (which would eventually host the [1928 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics")), Antwerp, Budapest, and Rome (which would eventually host the [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics")) were discussed. Despite a slight preference at the time for Budapest, no final choice was made, and the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") soon afterwards prevented any further progress.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1915, [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") made a bid for the 1920 Games, but after some discussion, they agreed to support Antwerp and postpone their bid until 1924 if Antwerp was liberated in time to organize the Games. The support for Belgium by cousin country France, then the leading country of the IOC, also meant that Amsterdam and Budapest (in an enemy state) had no chance for the 1920 games against Antwerp.

New candidacies from American cities did not have that disadvantage, and bids were received from [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), and [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") (which would eventually host the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics")), while Cuba also submitted a bid for [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana").

However, shortly after the armistice in November 1918, the IOC decided to give Antwerp their first preference, if they were still willing to host the 1920 Games. In March 1919, the Belgian Olympic Committee decided to go ahead with the organization of the Games: on 5 April 1919, at a meeting in Lausanne, Antwerp was officially declared the host city for the games of the VIIth Olympiad.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1920_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organization")]

The 1920 Summer Games organizers had very little time to prepare. The time between the IOC's decision of choosing Antwerp as the host city and the opening ceremony was 1 year, 4 months, and 9 days, making this the shortest time for a host city to plan and organize an Olympic edition.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

An executive committee was established on 17 April 1919, with Henri de Baillet-Latour as chairman and Alfred Verdyck, the secretary of the Belgian Union of Football Clubs, as general secretary. Seven commissions were created, to deal with finances, accommodation, press relations, propaganda, schedules, transport, and festivities. Finances and scheduling proved to be the two hardest parts to tackle: the program of events was not published until February 1920, six months before the official start of the Games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Between 23 and 30 April 1920, an ice hockey tournament marked the early start of the Games. Held in the "Palais de Glace" or Ice Palace in Antwerp, it was the first time that ice hockey was an Olympic sport.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The first stone of the new Olympisch Stadion was laid on 4 July 1919 by [Jan De Vos](/wiki/Jan_De_Vos_(politician) "Jan De Vos (politician)"), mayor of Antwerp, and inaugurated less than a year later on 23 May 1920 with a [gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") demonstration.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

When the Olympic Games began, the stadium was still unfinished with some events being built over fortifications and others using existing locations. The athletes quarters were crowded and athletes slept on folding cots.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The nautical stadium or [Stade Nautique d'Antwerp](/wiki/Stade_Nautique_d%27Antwerp "Stade Nautique d'Antwerp") was built at the end of the [Jan Van Rijswijcklaan](/w/index.php?title=Jan_Van_Rijswijcklaan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jan Van Rijswijcklaan (page does not exist)"), using the city ramparts there as a spectator's stand. Other events, like shooting, boxing, and equestrian sports, were held at pre-existing locations in and around Antwerp and as far away as [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The number of spectators was low throughout Antwerp's Summer Olympics, since not many people could afford tickets. In the closing days of the Olympic Games, students were allowed to attend the event for free.[[15]](#cite_note-15) After the conclusion of the Olympic Games, Belgium recorded a loss of more than 600 million francs.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1920_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Highlights")]

* The Olympic Games being a symbol of peace and global solidarity shone at Antwerp. These Olympics were the first in which the [Olympic Oath](/wiki/Olympic_Oath "Olympic Oath") was voiced, the first in which doves were released to symbolize peace, and the first in which the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag") was flown to display the unity of the world's continents through its 5 rings.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* The United States won 41 gold, 27 silver, and 27 bronze medals. Sweden, Great Britain, Finland, and Belgium rounded out the five most successful medal-winning nations, with France and Belgium being the nations that fielded the most athletes, with the United States being only the third by that statistic.
* The Games also featured a week of winter sports, with [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") appearing for the first time since the [1908 Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") making its Olympic debut.
* [Nedo Nadi](/wiki/Nedo_Nadi "Nedo Nadi") won 5 gold medals in the fencing events.
* At the age of 72, Sweden's [100 metre running deer double-shot](/wiki/100_metre_running_deer_double-shot "100 metre running deer double-shot") event champion [Oscar Swahn](/wiki/Oscar_Swahn "Oscar Swahn"), who had participated in the 1908 and 1912 Games, came in second in the team event to become the oldest Olympic medal winner ever.
* 23-year-old [Paavo Nurmi](/wiki/Paavo_Nurmi "Paavo Nurmi") won the 10,000 m and 8,000 m [cross country](/wiki/Cross_country_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Cross country at the 1920 Summer Olympics") races, took another gold in team cross country, and a silver in the 5,000 m run. His contributions for [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") broke a record in track and field with 9 medals.
* [Duke Kahanamoku](/wiki/Duke_Kahanamoku "Duke Kahanamoku") retained the 100 m swimming title he won before the war.
* The [sailing](/wiki/Sailing_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Sailing at the 1920 Summer Olympics") events were among some of strangest moments in Olympic history:[[18]](#cite_note-SAL-18)
  + there were originally 16 events scheduled but there were no entrants for the 9 metre, 1907 rating class nor the 8.5 metre 1919 rating class
  + the 12-foot dinghy event took place in two different countries. The final two races in the event were independently held in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), on its own accord, supposedly because the only two competitors in the event were Dutch.[[19]](#cite_note-ioc_games-19)
* [Sport shooter](/wiki/Sport_shooter "Sport shooter") [Guilherme Paraense](/wiki/Guilherme_Paraense "Guilherme Paraense") won [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")'s very first gold medal at the Olympic Games.
* The United States sent a women's swimming team for the first time, and the Americans won seven out of seven available swimming medals.

## Participating nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1920_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Participating nations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/1920_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png/250px-1920_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)](/wiki/File:1920_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)

Participants in the 1920 games, with the nations in blue participating for the first time.

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/1920_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png/250px-1920_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)](/wiki/File:1920_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)

Number of athletes

A total of 29 nations participated in the Antwerp Games, only one more than in 1912, as [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") and [Turkey](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") were not invited, having lost [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and sanctioned for starting it. From the newly created European states, only [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") took part, as [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") succeeded [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") and the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes") succeeded [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), with both nations had sent athletes prior to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (in the case of Bohemia as part of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire")). [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") was busy with the [Polish-Soviet War](/wiki/Polish-Soviet_War "Polish-Soviet War") and therefore was unable to form an Olympic team ([Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") in turn, had never participated in the games before, only doing so in later editions). [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") competed as nations at the Olympic Games for the first time. [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), which had competed as part of a combined team with Australia in 1908 and 1912, competed on its own for the first time. The games marked the return of [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and [India](/wiki/India "India") to the competitions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the time, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [India](/wiki/India "India") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") were all part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") was a British protectorate (a state not part of the British Empire but nonetheless administered by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")).
